translation: I swear by this city, Makkah
commentary:
لَآ أُقْسِمُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ

The surah begins with an emphatic oath: "I swear by this land." The Arabic construction لَآ أُقْسِمُ carries multiple layers of emphasis. The particle لَآ here functions not as a negation but as an intensifier, similar to saying "No, I swear" in English. This style implicitly challenges the audience, silencing objections before they're voiced, like a judge commanding attention in a courtroom.

Allah ﷻ doesn't simply say "the land" but rather "this land" (هَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ), pointing to something immediately present. This makes the audience conscious that they're standing in sacred territory as the oath is being revealed. Every listener knows without doubt that this refers to Makkah, the city where Ibrahim (AS) raised the foundations of the Kaaba, where the sacred house stands, where the center of faith for all humanity would be established.

The word بَلَد itself carries a beautiful duality. It can refer to an inhabited town but also to uninhabited wilderness. Makkah embodied both characteristics—it was a city, yet deeply underdeveloped, left in its pristine state. Throughout Islamic history, the ummah developed Baghdad, Damascus, Andalus and many other places with magnificent infrastructure, yet Makkah remained largely in its original form. This was by design—a recognition that sacred land should be left as it was, preserved in honor of its sanctity.

The Makkans weren't just any community. They were custodians of Allah's ﷻ greatest trust, the sacred house. If any nation should have been first in line to believe, it should have been them. Though their practices had become corrupted over generations—the pure monotheism of Ibrahim (AS) and Ismail (AS) mixed with idol worship—remnants of the original teachings remained. The rituals of Hajj were still performed. The Kaaba still stood. The water of Zamzam still flowed. These landmarks bore witness to the truth, waiting to be purified by the Quran's teachings.

When Allah ﷻ swears by this land, He's calling the Quraysh to remember what makes Makkah sacred: it's الحرم, the sanctuary where no fighting occurs, where even the animals can't be hunted, where the branches of trees can't be broken. This is land where everything is supposed to be protected and honored.

translation: And you, [O Muhammad], are free of restriction in this city
commentary:
وَأَنتَ حِلٌّۢ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْبَلَدِ

Before completing His oath, Allah ﷻ inserts a profound statement: "And you are permissible in this land." This ayah has been understood in several meaningful ways, each revealing a different dimension of the injustice being committed.

One interpretation is that it speaks to the future—eventually, the Prophet ﷺ will have free rein in this land. Right now he's oppressed and persecuted, but a time is coming when he'll do as he wishes without anyone holding him back or controlling him.

Another view is that حِلّ means he's a legitimate resident: "You are a resident of this city. You belong here just like everyone else. Why are they treating you as an outsider?" 

Some scholars see this as referring to Allah's ﷻ greatest blessing being "released" or "unleashed" in this land—the Prophet ﷺ himself, the final messenger, has been given as a mercy to this city.

However, the most compelling interpretation recognizes حِلّ as the direct opposite of حرم. This is المسجد الحرم, the sacred sanctuary where everything is protected and honored. Yet the Prophet ﷺ is being treated as حِلّ—fair game. They can spit at him, beat him, torture him, plot to kill him. In this land where nothing should be violated, they're violating the sanctity of the most sacred person among them.

This is the bitter irony Allah ﷻ exposes: in the one place on earth where everything should be treated with utmost respect, they treat the messenger of Allah ﷻ with utter contempt. Before He even completes His oath, He expresses His anger at this obscene contradiction.

translation: And [by] the father and that which was born [of him]
commentary:
وَوَالِدٍ وَمَا وَلَدَ

The oath continues: "And by a father and what he gave birth to"—or more accurately, "And by an amazing father and the amazing one he fathered." This يَا in وَمَا وَلَدَ is one of amazement and honor.

Given the context of this surah revolving around Makkah, its sacred history and rituals, this most likely refers to Ibrahim (AS) and his lineage, particularly leading to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Ibrahim (AS) founded this city when he left his family in this barren valley, where the discovery of Zamzam led to settlement. His legacy continued through Ismail (AS) and ultimately to the final prophet who would fulfill the prayer Ibrahim (AS) made while building the Kaaba: "Our Lord, send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses"(2:129).

The Prophet ﷺ is the truest heir to his father Ibrahim (AS), calling to the exact same truth that Ibrahim (AS) brought forth. And now they dare to persecute him, plot against him, attempt to drive him from this very land that exists because of Ibrahim's (AS) sacrifice and devotion? How dare they violate his sanctity when he embodies everything this sacred land was meant to represent?

translation: We have certainly created man into hardship
commentary:
لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِى كَبَدٍ

After these powerful oaths, Allah ﷻ makes a statement that seems unrelated at first: "We certainly created the human being in كَبَد." The word كَبَد comes from كَبِدَ, which refers to pain in the liver from working so hard that you develop physical problems—muscle aches, stomach issues, the exhaustion that comes from extreme exertion.

This is a profound universal truth: human beings will suffer in this life, one way or another. There is no escape from struggle and difficulty. Someone without money suffers from hunger and cold. Someone else suffers from illness, imprisonment or physical harm. Yet another lives in comfort with millions in the bank but can't sleep at night, drowning in anxiety, depression and emotional pain. 

Muslim, non-Muslim, atheist, believer, young, old, rich, poor—everyone faces their portion of difficulty. This is the inescapable reality of human existence.

How does this connect to what came before? There are two parties being addressed: the Quraysh who are persecuting the Prophet ﷺ because they can't accept his message, and the Prophet ﷺ himself who is being tortured unjustly. Allah ﷻ tells both of them: struggle and toil are the fate of humanity whether you believe or disbelieve. 

Those who reject faith won't find paradise in this life either. Those who chase pleasure still have pain. Those who chase wealth still have pain. To the Prophet ﷺ, Allah ﷻ is saying: this pain you're experiencing comes with the territory, but let it be the kind of pain that will pay off in the hereafter.

To the Quraysh, He's exposing a contradiction they can't deny: Life is full of trouble. No one will claim otherwise. Everyone accepts this reality. Yet having accepted it, how can they act as if they're completely in control, as if no one has power over them?

translation: Does he think that never will anyone overcome him
commentary:
أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّن يَقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ أَحَدٌ

"Does he think that no one has power over him?" This rhetorical question cuts to the heart of human arrogance. The Quraysh felt untouchable, especially in their persecution of the Prophet ﷺ. His grandfather had passed away, Abu Talib was aging—the Prophet ﷺ was increasingly vulnerable, an orphan without powerful tribal protection. They thought: Who will come for him if we go after him?

But the question extends beyond the Quraysh. Generally, despite all the difficulty and pain in life that should open people's eyes to their dependence on Allah ﷻ, human beings still act as if they're completely free, as if they have no accountability. They feel they have free rein to do whatever they want without consequences.

If you were truly in control, why can't you escape the misery that keeps hitting you? Why are you unable to prevent the difficulties that come back again and again? The very existence of struggle in your life proves you're not the one in charge.

translation: He says, "I have spent wealth in abundance
commentary:
يَقُولُ أَهْلَكْتُ مَالًا لُّبَدًا

"He says, 'I have destroyed abundant wealth.'" Now Allah ﷻ exposes their artificial religiosity. The Quraysh had a strange culture of generosity, particularly during Hajj season. They would gamble with each other—whoever lost the wager would have to pay for all the meat they'd slaughtered and distribute it in charity. Then they'd boast about it: "You know how much money I blew this year? You know how much I gave away?"

The word لُبَدًا (the plural of لِبْدَة) refers to thick bundles, like a thick braid of hair or fistfuls of coins. Picture someone flipping through wads of cash like a deck of cards, saying "Look how much I spent!" This was their way of doing "good"—wrapped in gambling, pride and showing off.

If they were genuinely charitable, they would never say how much they'd given. The fact that they broadcast it—أَهْلَكْتُ مَالًا لُّبَدًا—reveals their true motivation. This sacred land requires sincerity, leaving status and accomplishments behind. The whole notion of Hajj in the previous surah was يَأْتُوكَ رِجَالًا—come on foot, leave your pride behind, let it be just you and your Lord.

But these custodians of the House couldn't do good without making it a social PR stunt. Even their righteousness was corrupted.

translation: Does he think that no one has seen him
commentary:
أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّمْ يَرَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ

"Does he think that no one sees him?" This question works on multiple levels. Certainly Allah ﷻ sees everything—but the ayah also suggests: Don't you think people see right through your act? When you boast about your charity, do you think people are impressed? Or do they recognize it for the empty show it is?

People see through artificial displays. When politicians visit churches and kiss babies, nobody thinks "Oh, he loves babies." We see through it. Similarly, the Quraysh's ostentatious charity fooled no one. The word يَرَهُ encompasses both physical seeing and seeing through—perceiving the reality behind the facade.

Allah ﷻ is saying: You think you have something to criticize in the Prophet's ﷺ message? When the Quran exposes you so thoroughly, cutting to the heart of your corruption with just a few ayahs, leaving you speechless? This is one of the remarkable features of early Makkan Quran—it's like deep investigative journalism, exposing the most corrupt leaders in a way that leaves them dumbfounded, then turning to the people and saying: Don't you see what these people really are?

translation: Have We not made for him two eyes
commentary:
أَلَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عَيْنَيْنِ

Now comes a series of rhetorical questions that build upon each other: "Didn't We furnish him with two eyes?" This isn't just a generic reference to the blessing of sight. Allah ﷻ is highlighting what He's called them to see with those eyes.

Look at this land you're standing in—don't you recognize its sacredness? Look at this Messenger among you—don't you see his character is different from everyone else's? If you have eyes, use them. See who he really is. See what's in front of you.

translation: And a tongue and two lips
commentary:
وَلِسَانًا وَشَفَتَيْنِ

"And a tongue and two lips?" Why mention the tongue and then separately mention the lips? Because Allah ﷻ is making a pointed argument.

The word لِسَان comes from the root related to مِلْسَن—a trap used to catch animals. They would place meat on a door with a rock balanced above; when the animal pulled at the meat, the rock would fall and trap it. This reveals something profound: the Arabs understood that language at its essence is meant to captivate, to draw people in, to conquer hearts and minds with words. The most powerful weapon at humanity's disposal is language itself.

Now here's the devastating critique: The Quraysh prided themselves on their eloquence and mastery of speech. When the Prophet ﷺ came, he didn't bring an army or wealth or political power—he brought only words. The Quran. And the easiest way to defeat words is with words.

There were more of them. They could hold meetings and come up with better responses. But here's what they could not do, even though it should have been easy: come up with words that silence the Prophet ﷺ or overcome the Quran. If you have a tongue, why is it locked between your lips? Why don't you have a response?

The Quraysh were willing to violate the sanctity of the Kaaba—for which they're known across the land. They were willing to violate the constitution of their own tribe by spilling the blood of one of their own. But what they could not do was the one thing that required no bloodshed, no violation of sacred boundaries: simply respond to the Quran with words of their own.

Why don't you just open your mouth and respond? The fact that their tongues, which were supposedly so eloquent, remained silent—contained within their lips—is compelling evidence that they know they have no response. They know they've got nothing against the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

translation: And have shown him the two ways
commentary:
وَهَدَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلنَّجْدَيْنِ

"And We guided him to the two elevated paths." The word نَجْد refers to an elevated patch of land, not quite a mountain but a gradual progression upward. When you go up a hill, the first few steps are easy, but as you continue, your body takes a toll. You don't have the same energy you started with.

This captures the journey of life—you're winding down as you go up. The path gets harder, more exhausting, regardless of which direction you choose. It's the continuation of the theme from لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ فِى كَبَدٍ—whether you go toward Islam or toward disbelief, struggle will be there. Life is full of difficulty either way.

Allah ﷻ has shown both paths clearly. He hasn't forced anyone to take one or the other—He's shown you what lies ahead on each path. He's like a guide standing before you saying: "You can go up this hill, but there are deadly animals and quicksand. Or you can go up this other hill, and on the other side is a garden with your name on it." The guide has shown you both paths. The choice is yours.

This هَدَيْنَـٰهُ—We guided him—means We showed him, We made it clear to him. It's similar to إِنَّا هَدَيْنَـٰهُ ٱلسَّبِيلَ إِمَّا شَاكِرًا وَإِمَّا كَفُورًا (76:3)—"We guided him to the path, whether he be grateful or ungrateful, that's up to him."

There's another beautiful dimension to these three ayahs when read together. Think of a newborn child: it opens its eyes, then its tongue moves against its lips, and then Allah ﷻ guides it to النَّجْدَيْنِ—the two elevated places, the breasts of its mother. The child is instinctively guided to drink milk from the mother.

What does this parallel teach us? That Allah ﷻ endowed you with guidance from the very beginning. Even as an infant, you were guided to your mother's milk. As you grew, He continued to guide you—with your intellect, with those same eyes to see truth, with that tongue to ask questions if you don't understand. Instead of attacking the Prophet ﷺ, why not speak with him? You have a tongue—use it to seek truth.

translation: But he has not broken through the difficult pass
commentary:
فَلَا ٱقْتَحَمَ ٱلْعَقَبَةَ

"But no—he did not attempt the steep path." Now we come to the imagery that defines this surah's central message. The word عَقَبَة refers to a difficult, treacherous path heading sharply upward or downward, or winding up and down repeatedly. When you look at it, you think: nobody takes that road. That's عَقَبَة.

The word ٱقْتَحَمَ means to dive into something headfirst despite everyone telling you not to. Picture someone standing at the edge of a lake, all his friends saying "No, no, don't do it, you don't know how deep it is," and he just jumps in anyway. That's اقتحام—taking action where everyone else is saying "Don't do that, don't do that."

Remember the painting Allah ﷻ has been creating: He guided you to two hills. One seems smooth and easy, even though you'll be tired climbing it anyway. The other looks treacherous—few people have gone this way, and as you take the first step toward it, your friends, family and society say "No, no, no, that way is crazy. This way is so much easier."

This is the imagery of Islam. Islam seems like a very strange decision. The first steps into Islam seem insane to those around you. They make you think you're jumping into a ditch without knowing what's inside, diving into water without knowing what dangers lurk below. "Don't jump into Islam. You're being irrational."

And Allah ﷻ says: He heard all of this from people, and he decided "No, I'm not taking that leap. It's too much. I can't do it." فَلَا ٱقْتَحَمَ ٱلْعَقَبَةَ—he wasn't ready to take on Islam.

Notice the فَلَا here, which normally isn't used with past tense. This creates a "neither/nor" construction—neither did he take the leap, nor did he do what follows in the coming ayahs. It's a cascading series of things he failed to do.

If you follow the logic of this imagery to its conclusion, you arrive at a profound truth: Islam is a road that starts very difficult but ends only and only with ease. Kufr is a road that starts easy but ends only and only with difficulty.

translation: And what can make you know what is [breaking through] the difficult pass
commentary:
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْعَقَبَةُ

"And what will make you understand what the steep path is?" This is Allah's ﷻ way of building anticipation. We know it's Islam, but the surah itself now tells us: What's so difficult about this path that they can't accept it?

translation: It is the freeing of a slave
commentary:
فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ

"It is the freeing of a neck." The word رَقَبَة (neck) is used here instead of simply saying "freeing a slave" (تَحْرِير) because there are many kinds of chains and many kinds of necks—not just literal slavery.

فَكُّ رَقَبَةٍ means to break open the chains, to tear off the collar so the neck can be upright again. When someone is enslaved, they have a collar around their neck and their head is pulled down. To free the neck means to remove that collar so they can stand tall again.

But it's not just about literal slavery. There are people buried under debt. People buried under depression, abuse, oppression—their heads are down, they're drowning in sadness. The Arabs said: النَّاسُ عَبِيدُ الْإِحْسَانِ—"People are enslaved to goodness." A kind word can actually own someone's heart.

This is what's so hard about the path the Quraysh can't accept: they see people who are oppressed and they don't want to free them because freeing them means losing economic advantage. The Quraysh were economically manipulative. They needed to keep certain people downtrodden, low-income, enslaved, to keep the economy growing.

Some of the most unethical economies in the world thrive on underpaying employees, denying them rights. If workers gain too many rights, profits decrease. Someone gets sick? Deport them. Someone gets robbed by a citizen? They have no rights in court—deport them. Don't treat them like human beings.

This oppression existed in that region then, and tragically, remnants remain today. This road is still hard to travel. Allah ﷻ didn't say the hard thing about Islam is believing in one God or accepting the Quran. The criticism goes deeper: you can't treat people right. It's too much for you. It's not that you don't find the Quran convincing—you're just unwilling to give up your oppression of humanity.

translation: Or feeding on a day of severe hunger
commentary:
أَوْ إِطْعَـٰمٌ فِى يَوْمٍ ذِى مَسْغَبَةٍ

"Or feeding on a day of severe hunger." The word مَسْغَبَة means extreme hunger that comes with exhaustion—notice again the theme of exhaustion continues. It also means tough economic times.

In difficult economic times, people become less charitable. When things are hard, people think: "If I give this up, what about tomorrow? The economy isn't getting better. I might get laid off any day." Allah ﷻ is saying: the road I'm calling you to makes you not think of yourself first, but others first.

This calls back to the very beginning of the surah. Remember, He swore by this land—the land where Ibrahim (AS) stood, not for himself, but praying for others: رَبَّنَا إِنِّىٓ أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِى (14:37)—"Our Lord, I have settled some of my descendants..." His entire supplication was for others to be provided, to flourish. He constantly prayed for others, not himself.

The Quraysh, by contrast, are the embodiment of greed—concerned only with their own luxury. Allah ﷻ tells them: give food even when it's difficult for you. And to whom should you give?

translation: An orphan of near relationship
commentary:
يَتِيمًا ذَا مَقْرَبَةٍ

"An orphan of near relationship." Not just any orphan—an orphan from your own family, someone who lives near you, someone you see all the time. You still don't give him anything. You pass right by without even acknowledging this is an orphan.

This continues what we saw in the previous surahs: فَذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يَدُعُّ ٱلْيَتِيمَ (107:2)—"That is the one who pushes aside the orphan." They would consume the orphan's inheritance, and the orphan gets tossed aside. Who's going to fight for his rights? He doesn't have anyone to advocate for him.

By the way, the Messenger ﷺ himself is an orphan. What's being said to them applies first and foremost to him: Your messenger is an orphan, and you're supposed to be the greatest example in the world of taking care of the vulnerable.

translation: Or a needy person in misery
commentary:
أَوْ مِسْكِينًا ذَا مَتْرَبَةٍ

"Or a poor person in misery." The word مَتْرَبَة literally means "possessing dust"—someone covered in dirt, someone whose home is the dirt itself. This refers to someone who is homeless, someone who doesn't have enough to cover themselves, whose clothing is the dirt, who sleeps on the ground with nothing between their body and the earth.

This is who you should be caring for. But notice—the ayah already said مِسْكِينًا (poor person), so why add ذَا مَتْرَبَةٍ? It emphasizes the extremity of the condition. Not just poor, but in absolute destitution.

translation: And then being among those who believed and advised one another to patience and advised one another to compassion
commentary:
ثُمَّ كَانَ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْمَرْحَمَةِ

"And then being among those who believed and enjoined one another to patience and enjoined one another to mercy." The word ثُمَّ (then) here means "moreover" or "above and beyond." Being humanitarian is not enough. If you did all these good deeds, and then above and beyond all of that, you actually believed—that's when you've truly taken the steep path.

In other words: when you have your eyes open and see the poor around you (أَلَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُۥ عَيْنَيْنِ), your heart becomes soft toward humanity. And when your heart is soft toward humanity, then your heart will be ready to accept the teachings of Allah ﷻ.

This is critical: being a good person isn't the finish line—it's the preparation for faith. You can't see Allah ﷻ with your physical eyes, but you can see the poor around you. Start there. When your heart softens toward those you can see, you'll be ready to believe in the One you can't see.

Then comes وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ—they enjoin one another to patience. The word صَبْر means much more than patience. It comes from صَبَر, which refers to a tough rock that doesn't break. It means to keep building, keep moving forward without breaking apart, keep churning, keep progressing.

Go back to the image of climbing the hill. You're exhausted, but you encourage others: "Keep going, keep going, I got you, I got you." Then you get tired and they grab you: "Come on, I got you, I got you." This is تَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ—two people helping each other keep moving along, neither giving up.

There will be times when I'm harsh and my brother has to tell me "You need to be softer." There will be times when I'm giving up and my brother tells me "Come on, stay with it, you got this, we got this." There will be times when you slip and fall down the hill a little—you're going to make mistakes, you're going to fall into sin. Your brother can't just give up on you and say "You're done, I'm moving on without you." No—this is تَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ. Whoever is looking for a brother without flaws won't have a brother.

And وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْمَرْحَمَةِ—they enjoin one another to mercy. As you keep going, exhausted, there are still others in need. You keep reminding yourself and others: we have to keep caring for people. We have to have mercy toward them.

This is the call of this religion. This is the road up this hill.

Notice something beautiful: when Allah ﷻ mentions the tongue and lips earlier (وَلِسَانًا وَشَفَتَيْنِ), what would those lips open up for? They would open up for تَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلصَّبْرِ وَتَوَاصَوْا۟ بِٱلْمَرْحَمَةِ. Your tongue opens up to encourage others to take the tougher road, to hold on with grit, to keep showing mercy.

translation: Those are the companions of the right
commentary:
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَيْمَنَةِ

"Those are the companions of the right." The word مَيْمَنَة comes from يَمِين (right) and also from يُمْن (blessing). So this means both "people of the right hand" and "people of great blessing." 

The right hand represents honor—you shake hands with your right hand, take oaths with your right hand. These are people of honor. 

Think about how this connects to where the story began: this land is a land that must be honored. This messenger is a messenger who must be honored. And these are the people who, when they live up to this path, become people of honor themselves. They are the people of the right hand.

May Allah ﷻ make us among them.

translation: But they who disbelieved in Our signs - those are the companions of the left
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ

"And those who disbelieved in Our signs, they are the companions of the left." Notice something remarkable: was there any explicit mention of the Quran in this surah up to now? No direct mention of Quran, yet Allah ﷻ says "those who disbelieve in Our ayat."

What are the ayat of this surah? The orphan in front of you—denying that he deserves care is denial of an ayah of Allah ﷻ. The poor, the enslaved, the oppressed—denying their rights is denial of Allah's ﷻ signs. Those who don't encourage each other to do right—they are in denial of ayat.

وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا هُمْ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْمَشْـَٔمَةِ—these are people of misery. The word مَشْـَٔمَة means misery, but it also means a bad omen, a curse, an ugly mark that someone considers inauspicious.

Think about the contrast: this land has a pure, beautiful, profound history starting with Ibrahim (AS). But the Quraysh have surrounded that incredible pure history with their mythology, their superstitions, their fabricated stories. They'll be people of those mythologies, people of that misery. Allah ﷻ gave them ayat to clarify the history, but they chose to stick to their myths.

May Allah ﷻ not make us from among them.

translation: Over them will be fire closed in
commentary:
عَلَيْهِمْ نَارٌ مُّؤْصَدَةٌۢ

"Over them will be fire closed in." The word مُّؤْصَدَةٌ comes from أَصَدَ, which means to lock a door, to put a lid on something. When you have a fire and you put a lid on top of it, you're containing the heat—it has nowhere to be released. As the heat intensifies, there's no escape except for it to burn those trapped inside.

Look at the contrast: in the beginning, the word حِلّ was used—unleashed, opened. Now we have مُّؤْصَدَةٌ—closed up, sealed. This directly contrasts the opening of the surah.

The word also means an undergarment that covers tightly, suggesting the ugliness of hellfire will be contained—nobody gets to see what's inside. The horrors will be sealed upon them.

They used to think أَيَحْسَبُ أَن لَّمْ يَرَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ—"Does he think no one sees him?" Now, nobody will see them because they're inside a hell where the lid is closed over them. They wanted to hide from being seen, and now they got what they wanted—nobody cares to see them anymore. There's a lid on top of them.

May Allah ﷻ protect us from the fire from which nothing can escape.

---

# Reflecting on the Structure

Surah Al-Balad can be understood in three distinct parts that work together powerfully:

**The Current State** (Opening): The Prophet ﷺ must bear their crimes despite being in sacred land. Human beings are in constant struggle, yet the criminals feel no one has power over them and no one sees their deeds.

**The Call to Change** (Middle): Use the eyes Allah ﷻ gave you. Use your tongue for truth. Two pathways have been made clear—what's keeping you from the harder road? Free the enslaved. Feed the hungry and the orphan and the destitute. This is the steep path you must climb.

**The Future State** (End): Those who follow the Prophet's ﷺ way—believing, practicing patience, showing mercy to humanity—become people of the right hand, people of honor. Those who deny Allah's ﷻ signs become people of misery, sealed in a fire from which there's no escape.

At the heart of this structure is a profound message: the spirit of Makkah, the purpose of the Haram, is to soften the hearts of humanity toward humanity. People come to get close to Allah ﷻ, and those who serve them there should make them care about the rest of humanity. This is how they become witnesses to mankind—شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ.

This is why Ibrahim (AS) prayed رَبَّنَآ إِنِّىٓ أَسْكَنتُ مِن ذُرِّيَّتِى (14:37)—his entire supplication at the Kaaba was for others, for people to come and be provided for, for hearts to incline toward this place so they would leave with softened hearts toward all of creation.

When Allah ﷻ blesses you to go to Hajj or Umrah, don't eat a sandwich and throw the wrapper on the floor. If you see a wrapper, pick it up. If someone elbows you to reach the Black Stone, let them go forward—kissing that stone isn't more valuable than not elbowing an old person in the face. Your job is to become soft toward humanity when you go there. That is the legacy of our father Ibrahim (AS).

May Allah ﷻ give us true appreciation of His Book, help us understand it better and better, and allow us to draw from its endless ocean of wisdom.
